ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general frog meat food product class; no specific entry for griddle-cooked bullfrog. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers frog legs as the nearest available category; no specific griddle-cooked bullfrog entry exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for bullfrog or griddle-cooked frog meat as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of griddle cooked bullfrog in Singapore is approximately 350 g, served sizzling on a hot iron plate or in a claypot, shared between one to two diners.

nutrient_source.title: Frog, raw (NDB No. 17100) — USDA SR Legacy; adapted with cooking-loss and sauce-addition factors for griddle-cooked preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raw frog meat values from USDA SR Legacy (frog, raw, NDB 17100 / FDC ID 172944) were used as the base. Protein and fat values are consistent with published analyses of cooked frog meat. An upward adjustment of approximately 15% for fat was applied to account for cooking oil used in griddle preparation, and sodium was increased to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of this dish. Energy was recalculated from adjusted macros using Atwater factors. This is an adapted estimate; a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is not available in any approved primary source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant meat dishes — analogous estimate based on high-protein low-carbohydrate cooked meat foods; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for griddle-cooked bullfrog exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Bullfrog meat is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food (approximately 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g). By analogy with other high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes (GI range 20–40, typically assigned GI ~30 in the International Tables), a GI of 30 is used. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (30 × 9.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.9. This is a low-GI, low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1470 mg per 350 g serving) represents a significant proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Griddle cooked bullfrog is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with an estimated GI of 30 and glycemic load of approximately 2.9 per serving, making it a suitable protein choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with portion-controlled accompaniments and mindful of the sodium-rich sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from seasoning sauces and moderate fat from griddle cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While bullfrog meat itself is lean and provides a favourable protein-to-fat ratio, the high sodium content from soy and oyster sauces and the addition of cooking oil during griddle preparation elevate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose reduced-sodium sauce options and limit portion frequency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Griddle Cooked Bullfrog (铁板牛蛙, Tiě bǎn niú wā) is a popular Chinese dish served in Singapore and across Chinese-speaking communities in Southeast Asia. The dish features fresh bullfrog (commonly Hoplobatrachus rugulosus, the Chinese edible frog, or American bullfrog Rana catesbeiana) that is cleaned, jointed, and cooked on a very hot iron griddle or in a claypot with aromatics including garlic, ginger, chilli, spring onion, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The cooking method produces a sizzling, fragrant presentation with a slightly caramelised exterior and tender, moist flesh. Bullfrog meat has a mild, slightly sweet flavour often compared to chicken thigh meat, with a firm yet succulent texture. It is prized in Chinese culinary tradition for its high protein content and perceived health benefits. Nutritionally, griddle-cooked bullfrog is a high-protein (approximately 16 g per 100 g), moderate-energy (approximately 112 kcal per 100 g) dish with low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-glycaemic dietary patterns. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to the soy and oyster sauce seasoning. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy or shellfish (oyster sauce) allergies. In Singapore, it is commonly found in Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char (煮炒) stalls, often priced at a premium due to the cost of fresh bullfrog. It is typically shared as part of a multi-dish meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in iron plate bullfrog?
Is 铁板牛蛙 high in protein?
Where to eat sizzling bullfrog in Singapore?
Is griddle frog dish high in sodium?
What is tie ban niu wa?

confidence_reason: No exact match for griddle-cooked bullfrog was found in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw frog meat data with cooking and seasoning adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient data is from an approved source and the adaptation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw frog meat (FDC ID 172944) with adjustments for griddle cooking oil addition (~15% fat increase) and soy/oyster sauce sodium contribution. A direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is not available in any approved primary source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy frog meat entry. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy frog meat entry. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy frog meat entry. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) as frog meat contains negligible trans fats and no hydrogenated oils are used in traditional preparation; confirmed zero per USDA source. 6. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on high-protein low-carbohydrate cooked meat foods from the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). 7. The input food_group was listed as 'Grains and staples' which is incorrect for a meat dish; corrected to 'Meat and alternatives'. 8. Halal status is marked false as bullfrog is not universally accepted as halal under all Islamic jurisprudence schools; consumers should verify with their religious authority. 9. The field dataed (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles) is a Lanzhou noodle restaurant; the presence of griddle bullfrog on their menu is noted but does not affect nutritional classification.
